data.title: Fed's Bowman sees more risks on employment than inflation
data.description:
Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman said on Friday she is more concerned about the fragility of the labor market than about inflation resurging.

She estimates that, excluding the effect of tariffs, core PCE inflation is close to the Fed's target of 2%. "The underlying trend in core PCE inflation appears to be moving much closer to our 2% target than is currently showing in the data," she said in a speech. "Core services inflation is already roughly consistent with our target, and only core goods inflation remains elevated, but I expect it to start moving down in coming months as the effects of earlier price increases and one-time tariff-related adjustments fade."

Furthermore, less restrictive regulations, lower business taxes, and a more favorable business environment are likely to boost supply through higher productivity, offsetting any negative effects on economic activity and inflation from tariffs, she said.

However, Bowman continues to see downside risks to the Fed's full-employment mandate. "Continued softness in hiring, combined with already low hiring rates, means that even a small decline in demand could translate into a larger increase in unemployment," she said. "Once firms begin to shift from slowing hiring to reducing headcount, labor market conditions could quickly deteriorate."

She judges that current Fed policy is "moderately restrictive."

"Absent a clear and sustained improvement in labor market conditions, we should remain ready to adjust policy to bring it closer to "neutral"—the point at which its interest rate neither stimulates nor drags on economic activity, Bowman said.

"As the economy continues to evolve, policy must evolve with it. My focus will remain on acting early enough to preserve both price stability and a strong labor market," she concluded.

data.title: First Quantum says Panama to authorize ore removal at Cobre copper mine
data.description:
First Quantum Minerals (FQVLF) said Thursday that Panama's government will authorize the removal and processing of stockpiled ore at its shuttered Cobre Panama copper mine.

First Quantum (FQVLF) CEO Tristan Pascall said the move marks a positive step but does not constitute a reopening of the mine, and said the company is awaiting formal approval to carry out the activities in coordination with the government.

The company said the processing of ore stockpiles at the mine will mitigate environmental and operational risks associated with acid rock drainage and ensure a supply of feed material to the tailings management facility.

Panama's President said he expects to decide in the coming months whether to restart the mine, pending the outcome of an audit assessing the condition of the facilities and environmental risks.

The Cobre Panama mine, one of the world's largest open-pit copper deposits, was closed in 2023 following protests from local residents over tax contributions and environmental impacts, and its closure has hurt Panama's economy and First Quantum's (FQVLF) financial prospects.

data.title: U.S. stocks seesaw, even as some chipmakers advance
data.description:
Wall Street on Friday searched for direction, with losses in materials and utilities stocks offset by gains in some chipmakers and the industrial sector. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) struggled for momentum.

The Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was last -0.08%, while the S&P (SP500) edged down -0.06%. The blue-chip Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) fell -0.15%. 

Chipmakers extended their gains from the previous session in which they were buoyed by strong results from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and bargain hunting. Micron (MU) +6.17%, Lam Research (LRCX) +2.38%, and AMD (AMD) +2.23% were among the top percentage gainers on the Nasdaq (COMP:IND).

Over in the bond market, U.S. Treasury yields moved higher across the curve. The shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose to 3.60%, while the benchmark U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) climbed to 4.21%. The longer-end U.S. 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) increased to 4.82%.

Top S&P 500 (SP500) gainers included Super Micro Computer (SMCI) +7.48% and GE Vernova (GEV) +7.20%. Decliners included Constellation Energy (CEG) -9.71%, Vistra (VST) -6.69%, and Albemarle (ALB) -5.10%.

data.title: Mega-to-large cap utility stocks with the strongest earnings momentum
data.description:
As earnings season gets underway, attention turns to mega- and large-cap utility stocks showing strong earnings momentum. These names often draw investor interest, as upward revisions to analyst earnings estimates can point to improving underlying fundamentals.

The EPS revision rating measures how analysts have adjusted their earnings per share forecasts over recent periods, with positive revisions reflecting rising confidence in a company’s profit outlook. 

The following mega- to large-cap utility stocks have received top-tier EPS revision ratings, ranging from A+ to A-, signaling the most favorable trends in analyst earnings estimate revisions. 

  1. Engie SA (ENGIY) — EPS Revision Grade: A+
  2. Iberdrola, S.A. (IBDRY) — EPS Revision Grade: A+
  3. Atmos Energy Corporation (ATO) — EPS Revision Grade: A
  4. AXIA Energia SA (AXIA) — EPS Revision Grade: A
  5. Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP) — EPS Revision Grade: A 
  6. Sempra (SRE) — EPS Revision Grade: A
  7. Ameren Corporation (AEE) — EPS Revision Grade: A-
  8. Consolidated Edison, Inc. (ED) — EPS Revision Grade: A-
  9. Entergy Corporation (ETR) — EPS Revision Grade: A- 
  10. Exelon Corporation (EXC) — EPS Revision Grade: A- 
  11. NiSource Inc. (NI) — EPS Revision Grade: A- 
  12. Pinnacle West Capital Corporation (PNW) — EPS Revision Grade: A-

Utilities ETFs: (XLU), (VPU), (FUTY), (IDU), (FXU), and (JXI).

data.title: Trump to unveil plan for tapping 401(k)s for home down payment at Davos next week
data.description:
President Donald Trump will next week introduce a plan that would let 401(k) savers use some of the funds in their retirement plans for down payments on homes, National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said Friday in a broadcast interview.

"We’re going to allow people to take money out of their 401(k)s and use that for a down payment," he said on Fox Business's "Mornings with Maria" program. "We’re going to allow people to take money out of their 401(k)s and use that for a down payment."

President Trump will deliver a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, on Wednesday. The president had earlier said that he would announce at the event some initiatives to make housing more affordable for Americans.

Hassett said the mechanics for the plan are still being worked on. "Suppose that you put 10% down on a home, and then you take 10% of the equity of the home and put it in as an asset in your 401(k). Then your 401(k) will grow over time as the value of your house grows," he said.

Trump recently ramped up his initiatives to help make housing more affordable. Last week, he announced that he would ban large institutional investors from buying single-family houses. He also ordered Fannie Mae (FNMA) and Freddie Mac (FMCC) to buy $200B in mortgage bonds, which is expected to lower mortgage rates.

Earlier this month, Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte said the agency provided a list of 20 to 30 proposals aimed at making homes more affordable to the president, and that Trump will decide on which ones to pursue. Other ideas that were proposed last year include 50-year mortgages and portable mortgages.

On Thursday, Freddie Mac said the average 30-year fixed-rate mortgage rate was 6.06% as of Jan. 15, its lowest rate in about three years.

data.title: Trump says he doesn't want to lose Hassett in his current role
data.description:
President Donald Trump, in praising National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, said on Friday, "I actually want to keep you where you are, if you want to know the truth." 

Hassett is considered one of the frontrunners to be named the next Federal Reserve chair. Jerome Powell's term heading the central bank expires in May, and Trump has said he'll name his pick for Powell's successor in a few weeks.

The president said Hassett was “fantastic” on television on Friday. "If I move him, these Fed guys, certainly the ones we have now, they don't talk much. I would lose you. It's a serious concern to me," he said at the beginning of an event at the White House announcing the administration's plan for rural healthcare.

"We don't want to lose him, Susie," he said in a comment to his chief of staff, Susie Wiles. "We'll see how it all works out," he added.

In prediction markets, former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh is now favored to become the next Fed chair, with a 56% probability on Polymarket. Current Fed Governor Christopher Waller follows with 16% probability, then Hassett at 13%.

data.title: Diana Shipping launches proxy fight at Genco, nominates six directors
data.description:
Diana Shipping (DSX) said Friday it will launch a proxy fight against rival Genco Shipping (GNK), nominating six industry professionals for election to Genco's board at the upcoming shareholders meeting.

The move comes just days after Genco's (GNK) board rejected Diana's (DSX) $20.60/share takeover offer for the 85.2% of the company it does not already own, calling the offer inadequate.

Diana (DSX) said the current Genco (GNK) board has failed to engage constructively and meaningfully evaluate its aquisition proposal, and believes meaningful change is needed to ensure the board seriously evaluates its offer and other opportunities to maximize shareholder value.

Diana (DSX) said it was disappointed that Genco's (GNK) board took six weeks to respond to its offer and still believes in the merits of consolidation in the dry bulk carriers sector, and that shareholders would benefit if the two companies combined their platforms.

data.title: PureCycle and MP Materials top materials stocks in short interest; Greif and ICL show minimal exposure
data.description:
PureCycle Technologies (PCT) and MP Materials (MP) recorded the highest short interest among materials stocks as of Dec 31, 2025.

The materials sector comprises companies involved in chemicals, fertilizers, mining, and paper products. PureCycle Technologies (PCT) led the materials sector, with short interest at 23.80% of its shares outstanding, followed by MP Materials (MP) at 13.62%, reflecting elevated bearish positioning in both names.

PCT is rated Hold by the Seeking Alpha Quant model due to excessive valuation and poor profitability, coupled with weak momentum. MP Materials (MP), also rated Hold, has seen its shares advance 222.8% over the past year. Seeking Alpha Quant suggests MP's valuation is significantly stretched with its Price/Sales ratio at 44, far exceeding the sector median of 2.

At the other end of the spectrum, Greif’s Class B shares (GEF.B) and ICL Group (ICL) were among the least shorted materials stocks, with short interest of just 0.32% and 0.15%, respectively, pointing to relatively stronger investor confidence.

Here are the top 5 most shorted materials stocks, all with a market capitalization above $2 billion (as a % of shares outstanding)

  • PureCycle Technologies, Inc. (PCT): 23.80% short interest
  • MP Materials Corp. (MP): 13.62%
  • Alpha Metallurgical Resources, Inc. (AMR): 11.59%
  • Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. (CLF): 11.43%
  • Quaker Chemical Corporation (KWR): 11.27%

And here are the least shorted materials stocks, with a market capitalization above $2 billion:

  • Ardagh Metal Packaging (AMBP): 0.71% short interest
  • Aura Minerals Inc. (AUGO): 0.51%
  • Ternium S.A. (TX): 0.38%
  • Greif (GEF.B): 0.32%
  • ICL Group (ICL): 0.15%

More on materials stocks, etc.
